[[ja:御燈祭]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Winter events in the Kansai region]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Festivals in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Culture in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:February]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Oto Matsuri]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Old Lunar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Old Lunar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Old Lunar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Lunisolar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

revision.text:
[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 20qq.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 20qq.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 20qq.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 20qq.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}

Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>

[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|ja|2月6日}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|ja|注連縄}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
