revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489206]] Wikidata name: Otō Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on [[:en:February 6|February 6]], though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Kumano Chronology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]]). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]] around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)]], before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of festivals in Japan|List of Festivals of Japan]] * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)]]
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

<!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--> [[Category:Buddhist holidays]] [[Category:Festivals in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Culture of Shingu City]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Winter events in the Kinki region]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Oto-Matsuri}} {{wikidata link|Q11489206}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489206]] Wikidata name: Otō Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on [[:en:February 6|February 6]], though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Kumano Chronology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]]). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]] around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)]], before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489206]] Wikidata name: Otō Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on [[:en:February 6|February 6]], though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Kumano Chronology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]]). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]] around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)]], before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of festivals in Japan|List of Festivals of Japan]] * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)]]
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

[[ja:御燈祭]]
<!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--> [[Category:Buddhist holidays]] [[Category:Festivals in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Culture of Shingu City]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Winter events in the Kinki region]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Oto-Matsuri}} {{wikidata link|Q11489206}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489206]] Wikidata name: Otō Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on [[:en:February 6|February 6]], though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Kumano Chronology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]]). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]] around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)]], before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

<!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri-->
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Oto-Matsuri}} {{wikidata link|Q11489206}} [[Category:Buddhist holidays]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Culture of Shingu City]] [[Category:February]] [[Category:Festivals in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Winter events in the Kinki region]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489206]] Wikidata name: Otō Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on [[:en:February 6|February 6]], though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Kumano Chronology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]]). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]] around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)]], before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

[[ja:御燈祭]]
<!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Oto-Matsuri}} {{wikidata link|Q11489206}} [[Category:Buddhist holidays]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Culture of Shingu City]] [[Category:February]] [[Category:Festivals in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Winter events in the Kinki region]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489206]] Wikidata name: Otō Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on [[:en:February 6|February 6]], though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Kumano Chronology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]]). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]] around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and [[:en:Myōshin-ji|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)]], before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:Wikidata has English label]]" to "[[Category:qq]]"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489206]] Wikidata name: Otō Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|en|February 6|WD=Q2320|lt=February 6}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Kumano Chronology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|en|Shingū Station|WD=Q5357415|lt=Shingū Station}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myōshin-ji (Shingū)}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|WD=Q10312376|lt=List of Festivals of Japan}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myōshin-ji (Shingū)}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myōshin-ji (Shingū)}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]]
[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|en|February 6|WD=Q2320|lt=February 6}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Kumano Chronology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|en|Shingū Station|WD=Q5357415|lt=Shingū Station}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myōshin-ji (Shingū)}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Sakura, Man|year=1988|chapter=The Three Mountains' Festivals|editor2=Iida, Minoru|title=The Kumano Gongen|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=4480854215|ref={{SfnRef|Sakura|1988}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shirakawa, Takuma|year=1984|chapter=The Cosmology of Kumano Festivals|others2=Miyake, Jun|title=The Mountain Festivals and Performing Arts|volume=Vol. 1|publisher=Heikawa Shuppansha|isbn=4892030791|ref={{SfnRef|Shirakawa|1984}}}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025|ref={{SfnRef|Heibonsha Editorial|1997}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|WD=Q10312376|lt=List of Festivals of Japan}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myōshin-ji (Shingū)}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|1=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|en|Shingū Station|WD=Q5357415|lt=Shingū Station|1=Shingū Station}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|1=Myōshin-ji}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}

[[ja:御燈祭]]
<!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--><!--Oto Matsuri--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Oto-Matsuri}} {{wikidata link|Q11489206}} [[Category:Buddhist holidays]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Culture of Shingu City]] [[Category:February]] [[Category:Festivals in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Winter events in the Kinki region]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Oto-Matsuri]][[ja:御燈祭]]
[[File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Scene from the Oto Matsuri (2016). Only men in formal attire, dressed in white garments, tabi socks, straw sandals, and wrapped with rope around the waist, carrying torches (called Noboriko), are allowed to receive the sacred fire at the mountain’s summit.<ref name=moriyasu>[https://uuair.lib.utsunomiya-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10241/7604/1/61-1-moriyasu.pdf Kenji Nakagami's "Fire Festival": From Film to Novel] Moriyasu Toshihisa, Bulletin of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education No. 61, March 2011.</ref>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|250px|The worship hall of Kamikura Shrine and the sacred Gotobiki Rock. Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|en|February 6|WD=Q2320|lt=February 6|1=February 6}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Q11568915|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|1=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|en|Shingū Station|WD=Q5357415|lt=Shingū Station|1=Shingū Station}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|1=Myōshin-ji}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
== References == ;Notes {{Reflist|group="注"}} ;Citations {{Reflist|2}}

== External Links == * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/053.html Kumano Oto Matsuri] - Wakayama Prefecture Photo Museum * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200030_00000 The Oto Matsuri: A Majestic Fire Festival Continuing for 1,400 Years] - NHK

Approximately 2,000 Noboriko gather in the confined precincts, waiting for the arrival of the sacred fire after the Tama-gaki gate is closed.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] The '''Oto Matsuri''' (御燈祭, also spelled 御灯祭 or お灯祭) is a vibrant fire festival and the annual ritual of {{ill|Kamikura Shrine|ja|神倉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikura Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. Renowned as a bold and dynamic event, it has been designated a {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan (March 2, 2016, alongside the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja|速玉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 40, March 2, 2016.</ref>
== A Festival to Welcome the New Year's Fire == The Oto Matsuri is held annually on {{ill|February 6|en|February 6|WD=Q2320|lt=February 6|1=February 6}}, though it was traditionally observed on the 6th day of the {{ill|Old Japanese lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Japanese lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} (according to the "{{ill|Kii Zoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Zoku Fudoki|qq=}}").<ref>{{Sfn|Heibonsha Editorial|1997|p=192}}</ref> Historically, it signified the renewal of fire for the New Year, with homes forbidden from lighting lamps until they received the sacred fire distributed during the festival.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=180-181}}</ref>
The festival's origins are traced to records in the "{{ill|Kumano Chronology|ja|熊野年代記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Chronology|1=Q11568915|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568915}}" from Emperor Bidatsu's era, noting the beginning of the fire festival in 574 CE. Local tradition connects it to the myth of {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|1=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}}, where {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} greeted Emperor Jimmu with a torch to guide him to the Kumano region.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=181}}</ref>
== Ritual Schedule == [[File:Otō Festival Monument.jpg|thumb|Monument commemorating the Oto Matsuri (in front of {{ill|Shingū Station|en|Shingū Station|WD=Q5357415|lt=Shingū Station|1=Shingū Station}}). Noboriko purify themselves for a week before the festival, abstaining from women and eating only white foods such as rice, tofu, and white fish.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] [[File:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|200px|The steep stone staircase of Kamikura Shrine. Only Noboriko are permitted to ascend and descend the 538 steps, carrying torches as they participate in the ritual.<ref name=moriyasu/>]] Participation is limited to men, who must undergo a week of purification (shōjin kessai) before the festival, abstaining from women and consuming only white foods like rice and tofu. During this period, the {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}} around Gotobiki Rock is replaced.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|pp=178-179}}</ref>
On the festival day, participants called Noboriko appear in white garments, wrapped in rope, and carry torches filled with shavings of hinoki wood. They visit {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|WD=Q376288|lt=Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|1=Myōshin-ji}}, before ascending Kamikura Shrine.<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=178}}</ref>
At the summit, Noboriko light their torches from the sacred fire and descend the 538 steep stone steps in a breathtaking spectacle. The rapid descent, accompanied by the fiery glow, is likened to a "descending dragon."<ref>{{Sfn|Shirakawa|1984|p=180}}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:519wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Receiving the sacred fire on a pentagonal torch, believed to purify participants.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Noboriko rushing down the steps with the sacred fire.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:517wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Honoring fire's ancient role as both revered and feared.<ref name=moriyasu/> File:520wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|Reflecting ancient nature worship and sacred rituals.<ref name=moriyasu/> </gallery>
